program expr2
    implicit none
    print*, min("string_var", "hello")
end program
```
```console
$ lfortran a.f90
ASR verify pass error: ASR verify: ASR Verify: Arguments to min0 must be of real or integer type
 --> a.f90:3:13
  |
3 |     print*, min("string_var", "hello")
  |             ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ failed here


Note: Please report unclear or confusing messages as bugs at
https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues.
```
The `ASR verify` repeats twice as in the error above
`ASR verify pass error: ASR verify: ASR Verify: Arguments to min0 must be of real or integer type`

`lfortran` version
```
LFortran version: 0.42.0
Platform: Linux
LLVM: 19.1.1
Default target: x86_64-conda-linux-gnu
```
lfortran will exceed the character len
- code
```fortran
program main
   implicit none
    character(len=1)::s(2)
    read(*,*)s
    print*,s(1)
    print*,s(2)
end program main
```
``` sh
$ gfortran 1.f90 && ./a.out
ab cd
a
c
$lfortran 1.f90
ab cd
ab
cd
```

of #3770 

We now free allocatables inside `StructType` like:

```fortran
program main
    type my_type
        integer, allocatable :: int(:)
    end type

    type(my_type) :: x

    allocate(x%int(2))
end program
```

Along with this, we also free other scalar allocatables like `integer, allocatable :: Ah, by this I had meant array item of array `arr` where `arr` is an allocatable. Looks like we do not support this case currently. For now, we can open up an issue for this and use this fix for continuing with PRIMA.

```fortran
program array_indices_array_item

    implicit none

    integer, allocatable ::  arr(:, :)
    integer :: arr_idx(2)

    allocate(arr(1, 3))

    arr = reshape([1, 2, 3], shape(arr))
    arr_idx = [2, 1]

    print *, arr(arr_idx, 1)

end program
```

#4420 

We convert an `ArrayItem` like `x(loc, loc)`, where `loc` is an array, to:

```fortran
do __1_t = lbound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 1), ubound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 1)
    do __2_t = lbound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 2), ubound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 2)
        __libasr__created__var__0__array_item(__1_t, __2_t) = x(loc(__1_t), loc(__2_t))
    end do
end do
```

#4420 

We convert an `ArrayItem` like `x(loc, loc)`, where `loc` is an array, to:

```fortran
do __1_t = lbound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 1), ubound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 1)
    do __2_t = lbound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 2), ubound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 2)
        __libasr__created__var__0__array_item(__1_t, __2_t) = x(loc(__1_t), loc(__2_t))
    end do
end do
```

`lfortran` version
```
LFortran version: 0.42.0
Platform: Linux
LLVM: 19.1.1
Default target: x86_64-conda-linux-gnu
```
lfortran will exceed the character len
- code
```fortran
program main
   implicit none
    character(len=1)::s(2)
    read(*,*)s
    print*,s(1)
    print*,s(2)
end program main
```
``` sh
$ gfortran 1.f90 && ./a.out
ab cd
a
c
$lfortran 1.f90
ab cd
ab
cd
```
 if use `character(len=1)::s` ,it is right","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5545/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5545/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2513661667","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5545#issuecomment-2513661667","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5545","id":2513661667,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCpMuHc6V027j","user":{"login":"kmr-srbh","id":151380951,"node_id":"U_kgDOCQXj1w","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/151380951?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh","html_url":"https://github.com/kmr-srbh","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-03T06:22:25Z","updated_at":"2024-12-03T06:22:25Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"> ```fortran

>    print*,s,len(s)

> 123    3

Thanks for this @Euler37. This is indeed an issue with formatted read.

> and for previous example
> 
> ```fortran
> program main
>    implicit none
>     character(len=1)::s(2)
>     read(*,*)s
>     print*,s(1),len(s(1))
>     print*,s(2),len(s(2))
> end program main
> ```
> 
> it will show
> 
> ```shell
> ab cd
> ab    2
> cd    2
> ```

```
LFortran version: 0.42.0
Platform: Linux
LLVM: 19.1.1
Default target: x86_64-conda-linux-gnu
```
lfortran will exceed the character len
- code
```fortran
program main
   implicit none
    character(len=1)::s(2)
    read(*,*)s
    print*,s(1)
    print*,s(2)
end program main
```
``` sh
$ gfortran 1.f90 && ./a.out
ab cd
a
c
$lfortran 1.f90
ab cd
ab
cd
```

```fortran 
module gg
    contains
    function func_arr3(inp_ref) result(ret_arr)
        integer, intent(in) :: inp_ref
        integer,allocatable :: ret_arr(:)
        ! print *, allocated(ret_arr)
        allocate(ret_arr(1))
        ret_arr = [inp_ref]
        print *, "worked", inp_ref
    end function
end module


program p
    use gg
    implicit none
    integer, allocatable :: inp(:)
    integer :: i =77
    
    do i= 1, 3
        inp = func_arr3(i)
    end do 
end program  
end program

This happened because we always import `ExternalSymbol` to the symbol table based on `m_original_name`, which in this case, was same.

With the changes in this PR, we use the symbol name for importing the symbol if it is a function as it can be part of a `GenericProcedure` and have the same name. We thus avoid the function from getting overwritten.

the following code:
```fortran
module fortuno_basetypes
   implicit none

   type, abstract :: test_base
      character(:), allocatable :: name
   end type test_base

   type, extends(test_base) :: test_base_derived
   end type test_base_derived


   type :: test_ptr_item
      class(test_base), pointer :: item => null()
   end type test_ptr_item


   type :: test_list
      type(test_ptr_item), pointer :: storage_(:) => null()
      integer :: nitems = 0
   contains
      procedure :: free => test_list_free
   end type


contains

   subroutine test_list_free(this)
      class(test_list), intent(inout) :: this

      select type (item => this%storage_(1)%item)
       type is (test_base_derived)
         print *, "type is test_base"
       class default
      end select

   end subroutine test_list_free

end module fortuno_basetypes

program fortuno_01
   use fortuno_basetypes
   implicit none

   type(test_list) :: my_list
   allocate(my_list%storage_(1))
   allocate(test_base_derived::my_list%storage_(1)%item)

   call my_list%free()

   print *, "Test list has been freed successfully."
end program
```

The output is:
```console
(lf) saurabh-kumar@Awadh:~/Projects/System/lfortran$ gfortran ./examples/example.f90 && ./a.out
 type is test_base
 Test list has been freed successfully.
(lf) saurabh-kumar@Awadh:~/Projects/System/lfortran$ lfortran ./examples/example.f90
code generation error: test_base doesn't have any member named storage_
  --> ./examples/example.f90:30:28
   |
30 |       select type (item => this%storage_(1)%item)
   |                            ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ 


Note: Please report unclear, confusing or incorrect messages as bugs at

operations."}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-12-04T16:49:56Z","updated_at":"2024-12-04T22:00:37Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"MRE:
```fortran
program file_read
implicit none
integer :: rows, cols, i
character(len=:), allocatable :: lines(:)
integer :: u
open(newunit=u, file="input.txt", status='old')
rows = 2
cols = 2
print *, rows, cols
allocate(character(len=cols) :: lines(rows))
do i = 1, rows
    print *, i
    read(u, '(A)') lines(i) ! fails here
    print *, lines(i)
end do
close(u)
end program
```
Here is the `input.txt`:
```
xx
yy
```
In GFortran:
```console
$ gfortran a.f90 && ./a.out 
           2           2
           1
 xx
           2
 yy
```
And LFortran:
```console
$ lfortran a.f90 
2    2
1
```

The following diff makes it work:
```diff
--- a/a.f90
+++ b/a.f90
@@ -2,6 +2,7 @@ program file_read
 implicit none
 integer :: rows, cols, i
 character(len=:), allocatable :: lines(:)
+character(len=100) :: tmp
 integer :: u
 open(newunit=u, file="input.txt", status='old')
 rows = 2
@@ -10,7 +11,8 @@ print *, rows, cols
 allocate(character(len=cols) :: lines(rows))
 do i = 1, rows
     print *, i
-    read(u, '(A)') lines(i) ! fails here
+    read(u, '(A)') tmp ! fails here
+    lines(i) = tmp
     print *, lines(i)
 end do
 close(u)
```
Then:
```console
$ gfortran a.f90 && ./a.out
           2           2
           1
 xx
           2
 yy
$ lfortran a.f90           
2    2
1
xx
2
yy
```

So the bug is just reading into an allocatable array of strings. Reading into just one string and then assigning to the allocatable array works.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5575/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5575/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2518648235","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5575#issuecomment-2518648235","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5575","id":2518648235,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCpMuHc6WH4Wr","user":{"login":"kmr-srbh","id":151380951,"node_id":"U_kgDOCQXj1w","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/151380951?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh","html_url":"https://github.com/kmr-srbh","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-04T22:00:36Z","updated_at":"2024-12-04T22:00:36Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"We need to initialize the character array elements with empty strings in order to support this. Main issue: #5566 ","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5598/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":2,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5598/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2524153842","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5598#issuecomment-2524153842","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5598","id":2524153842,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCpMuHc6Wc4fy","user":{"login":"kmr-srbh","id":151380951,"node_id":"U_kgDOCQXj1w","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/151380951?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh","html_url":"https://github.com/kmr-srbh","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-06T20:51:15Z","updated_at":"2024-12-06T20:51:15Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I think we have support for using structs before declaration. We handle it as `ExternalSymbol` in `ast_common_visitor.h`. It was added in #1280 for compiling FPM and was meant to address the case where we declare a variable inside struct B using struct A, but struct A itself is declared later.

We can build on top of it and support variable declarations where we point to the same struct. The following diff gets this working for me, although I must admit that I do not understand the significance of `self` in Fortran and can be completely off here.

```diff
diff --git a/src/lfortran/semantics/ast_symboltable_visitor.cpp b/src/lfortran/semantics/ast_symboltable_visitor.cpp
index aa781e622..377ac5d14 100644
--- a/src/lfortran/semantics/ast_symboltable_visitor.cpp
+++ b/src/lfortran/semantics/ast_symboltable_visitor.cpp
@@ -1854,7 +1854,7 @@ public:
         }
         SetChar struct_dependencies;
         struct_dependencies.reserve(al, 1);
-        for( auto& item: current_scope->get_scope() ) {
+        for( auto item: current_scope->get_scope() ) {
             // ExternalSymbol means that current module/program
             // already depends on the module of ExternalSymbol
             // present inside Struct's scope. So the order
@@ -1873,6 +1873,27 @@ public:
                     ASR::symbol_t* sym = ASR::down_cast<ASR::ClassType_t>(var_type)->m_class_type;
                     aggregate_type_name = ASRUtils::symbol_name(sym);
                 }
+                // Check for data member pointing to the parent struct itself
+                if (ASR::is_a<ASR::Variable_t>(*item.second)
+                    && ASR::is_a<ASR::StructType_t>(
+                        *ASRUtils::type_get_past_array_pointer_allocatable(var_type))) {
+                    ASR::Variable_t* var = ASR::down_cast<ASR::Variable_t>(item.second);
+                    // We check for an ExternalSymbol because we declare it as this when we use a
+                    // Struct before declaration, see `determine_type()` inside `ast_common_visitor.h`
+                    if (ASR::is_a<ASR::ExternalSymbol_t>(
+                        *ASR::down_cast<ASR::StructType_t>(
+                                ASRUtils::type_get_past_array_pointer_allocatable(var->m_type))
+                                ->m_derived_type)) {
+                        // If m_derived_type of StructType is an ExternalSymbol, we can be sure that we
+                        // are using a Struct before declaration, so we point to its parent scope
+                        ASR::down_cast<ASR::ExternalSymbol_t>(
+                            ASR::down_cast<ASR::StructType_t>(
+                                ASRUtils::type_get_past_array_pointer_allocatable(
+                                    var->m_type))->m_derived_type)->m_parent_symtab = parent_scope;
+                        // Update the variable after change in type above
+                        item.second = &var->base;
+                    }
+                }
             }
             if( aggregate_type_name ) {

`predsci/POT3D`"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-12-13T18:43:47Z","updated_at":"2024-12-14T12:57:25Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"## Minimal Reproducible Example

For the below Fortran program:
```f90
module meshdef
    implicit none
    integer, dimension(2) :: drratio=0 ! if this was [1, 2] it works
end module

program main
    implicit none
end program main

```

on executing it with LFortran, I get the below error:
```console
> lfortran test.f90
; ModuleID = 'LFortran'
source_filename = "LFortran"

@drratio = global [2 x i32] zeroinitializer

define i32 @main(i32 %0, i8** %1) {
.entry:
  call void @_lpython_call_initial_functions(i32 %0, i8** %1)
  call void @_lpython_free_argv()
  br label %return

return:                                           ; preds = %.entry
  ret i32 0
}

declare void @_lpython_call_initial_functions(i32, i8**)

declare void @_lpython_free_argv()
asr_to_llvm: module failed verification. Error:
Global variable initializer type does not match global variable type!
[2 x i32]* @drratio

code generation error: asr_to_llvm: module failed verification. Error:
Global variable initializer type does not match global variable type!
[2 x i32]* @drratio



Note: Please report unclear, confusing or incorrect messages as bugs at
https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues.
```

while the above program works without error with GFortran.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5690/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5690/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2543099284","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5690#issuecomment-2543099284","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5690","id":2543099284,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCpMuHc6XlJ2U","user":{"login":"kmr-srbh","id":151380951,"node_id":"U_kgDOCQXj1w","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/151380951?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh","html_url":"https://github.com/kmr-srbh","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-14T12:57:24Z","updated_at":"2024-12-14T12:57:24Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"The issue here is related specifically to initializing with `0`. This is a special case because of the way LLVM handles it. When we initialize the array in the above MRE, we loop over the elements in the `ArrayConstant` and add to a vector as `llvm::Constant*`. Post this we initialize the global variable with a `llvm::ConstantArray` created from the same vector. In the above case, that vector contains only 0s, so LLVM initializes the variable with `zeroinitializer` instead of `[i32 0, i32 0]`. The following diff mitigates the issue:
```diff
diff --git a/src/libasr/codegen/asr_to_llvm.cpp b/src/libasr/codegen/asr_to_llvm.cpp
index 3c2dddb8f..a8da0e4c2 100644
--- a/src/libasr/codegen/asr_to_llvm.cpp
+++ b/src/libasr/codegen/asr_to_llvm.cpp
@@ -2791,7 +2791,14 @@ public:
                         }
                     }
                     llvm::ArrayType* arr_type = llvm::ArrayType::get(type, arr_const_size);
-                    module->getNamedGlobal(x.m_name)->setInitializer(llvm::ConstantArray::get(arr_type, arr_elements));
+                    if (llvm::ConstantArray::get(arr_type, arr_elements)
+                            ->isElementWiseEqual(llvm::ConstantArray::getNullValue(arr_type))) {
+                        module->getNamedGlobal(x.m_name)->setInitializer(
+                            llvm::ConstantArray::getNullValue(type));
+                    } else {
+                        module->getNamedGlobal(x.m_name)->setInitializer(
+                            llvm::ConstantArray::get(arr_type, arr_elements));
+                    }
                 } else {
                     module->getNamedGlobal(x.m_name)->setInitializer(llvm::ConstantArray::getNullValue(type));
                 }
```

But, I think the correct fix should be to handle this case in the ASR, where we convert the above declaration to `integer, dimension(2) :: drratio`. LLVM will then directly assign a `zeroinitializer` to this instead of an array of 0s as the above will be very slow when we have a very large array.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2543099284/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5707","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5707/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5707/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5707/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5707","id":2740383295,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc6jVu4_","number":5707,"title":"allocate statement disallowed!","user":{"login":"harperjf","id":25044934,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI1MDQ0OTM0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/25044934?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf","html_url":"https://github.com/harperjf","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/harperjf/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-12-15T07:51:08Z","updated_at":"2024-12-15T09:26:46Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"This program uses integer arrays ```x(0:2)``` and allocatable ```y(:)```, which is allocated with the same bounds.
```
  integer x(0:2)
  integer,allocatable::y(:)
  x(:) = [1,2,3]
  x(1:) = [10,20]
  print "(3(1X,I0))",x
  allocate(y(0:2))
  y(:) = [1,2,3]
  y(1:) = [10,20]
  print "(3(1X,I0))",y
end program
```
Lfortran 0.42.0 misinterorets the allocate statement.
```
 + lfortran --std=f23 -o badarray.f90oL badarray.f90
syntax error: Statement or Declaration expected inside program, found Expression
 --> badarray.f90:6:3
  |
6 |   allocate(y(0:2))
  |   ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ 
```
Run-time outputs from gfortran, g95, ifort, ifx and AMD flang are identical and correct:
```
 1 10 20
 1 10 20
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5707/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5707/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2543636543","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5707#issuecomment-2543636543","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5707","id":2543636543,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCpMuHc6XnNA_","user":{"login":"kmr-srbh","id":151380951,"node_id":"U_kgDOCQXj1w","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/151380951?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh","html_url":"https://github.com/kmr-srbh","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-15T09:26:44Z","updated_at":"2024-12-15T09:26:44Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Thanks for reporting this @harperjf! of #3770 

We now free allocatables inside `StructType` like:

```fortran
program main
    type my_type
        integer, allocatable :: int(:)
    end type

    type(my_type) :: x

    allocate(x%int(2))
end program
```

Along with this, we also free other scalar allocatables like `integer, allocatable :: int`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5486/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5486/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2543775635","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/pull/5486#issuecomment-2543775635","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5486","id":2543775635,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCpMuHc6Xnu-T","user":{"login":"kmr-srbh","id":151380951,"node_id":"U_kgDOCQXj1w","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/151380951?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh","html_url":"https://github.com/kmr-srbh","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-15T10:27:03Z","updated_at":"2024-12-15T10:27:03Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"The current failure is due to the `simplifier` pass. {"action":"opened","number":5708,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/pulls/5708","id":2235718092,"node_id":"PR_kwDOCpMuHc6FQlnM","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/pull/5708","diff_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/pull/5708.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/pull/5708.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5708","number":5708,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"fix: add `AST` node for `allocate` in programs without `program` line","user":{"login":"kmr-srbh","id":151380951,"node_id":"U_kgDOCQXj1w","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/151380951?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh","html_url":"https://github.com/kmr-srbh","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"fixes #5707

- When no `program` line is used, we handle the `allocate` statement as a general `AST::FuncCallOrArray_t` node, which is also a `AST::expr_t`.

- Previously, we handled `AST::expr_t` only for `AST::Name_t` and skipped this case, causing errors.

- In the current implementation, we create the required `AST::Allocate_t` node and add to the program line","user":{"login":"kmr-srbh","id":151380951,"node_id":"U_kgDOCQXj1w","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/151380951?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh","html_url":"https://github.com/kmr-srbh","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"fixes #5707

- When no `program` line is used, we handle the `allocate` statement as a general `AST::FuncCallOrArray_t` node, which is also a `AST::expr_t`.

- Previously, we handled `AST::expr_t` only for `AST::Name_t` and skipped this case, causing errors.

- In the current implementation, we create the required `AST::Allocate_t` node and add to the program #5690 
In this PR i have changed 
```fortran
integer, dimension(2) :: drratio=0 --------> integer,dimension(2) :: drratio
real, dimension(2) :: drratior=0.0 ---------> real, dimension(2) :: drratior
```
On ASR level 
Which prevents this llvm error 
```clojure
aditya-trivedi   lfortran    main ≡    lfortran integration_tests/array_init_with_scalar.f90 --show-llvm
; ModuleID = 'LFortran'
source_filename = "LFortran"

@drratio = global [2 x i32] zeroinitializer
@drratior = global [2 x float] zeroinitializer

define i32 @main(i32 %0, i8** %1) {
.entry:
  call void @_lpython_call_initial_functions(i32 %0, i8** %1)
  call void @_lpython_free_argv()
  br label %return

return:                                           ; preds = %.entry
  ret i32 0
}

declare void @_lpython_call_initial_functions(i32, i8**)

declare void @_lpython_free_argv()
asr_to_llvm: module failed verification. Error:
Global variable initializer type does not match global variable type!
[2 x i32]* @drratio
Global variable initializer type does not match global variable type!
[2 x float]* @drratior

code generation error: asr_to_llvm: module failed verification. Error:
Global variable initializer type does not match global variable type!
[2 x i32]* @drratio
Global variable initializer type does not match global variable type!
[2 x float]* @drratior



Note: Please report unclear, confusing or incorrect messages as bugs at
https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues.

```

#5690 
In this PR i have changed 
```fortran
integer, dimension(2) :: drratio=0 --------> integer,dimension(2) :: drratio
real, dimension(2) :: drratior=0.0 ---------> real, dimension(2) :: drratior
```
On ASR level 
Which prevents this llvm error 
```clojure
aditya-trivedi   lfortran    main ≡    lfortran integration_tests/array_init_with_scalar.f90 --show-llvm
; ModuleID = 'LFortran'
source_filename = "LFortran"

@drratio = global [2 x i32] zeroinitializer
@drratior = global [2 x float] zeroinitializer

define i32 @main(i32 %0, i8** %1) {
.entry:
  call void @_lpython_call_initial_functions(i32 %0, i8** %1)
  call void @_lpython_free_argv()
  br label %return

return:                                           ; preds = %.entry
  ret i32 0
}

declare void @_lpython_call_initial_functions(i32, i8**)

declare void @_lpython_free_argv()
asr_to_llvm: module failed verification. Error:
Global variable initializer type does not match global variable type!
[2 x i32]* @drratio
Global variable initializer type does not match global variable type!
[2 x float]* @drratior

code generation error: asr_to_llvm: module failed verification. Error:
Global variable initializer type does not match global variable type!
[2 x i32]* @drratio
Global variable initializer type does not match global variable type!
[2 x float]* @drratior



Note: Please report unclear, confusing or incorrect messages as bugs at
https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues.

changes"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-12-15T12:50:21Z","updated_at":"2024-12-15T12:50:21Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Minimal Reproducible Example

```fortran
program main
   integer :: x=2
end program
```

### ASR

```clojure
(TranslationUnit
    (SymbolTable
        1
        {
            main:
                (Program
                    (SymbolTable
                        2
                        {
                            x:
                                (Variable
                                    2
                                    x
                                    []
                                    Local
                                    (IntegerConstant 2 (Integer 4) Decimal)
                                    (IntegerConstant 2 (Integer 4) Decimal)
                                    Save
                                    (Integer 4)
                                    ()
                                    Source
                                    Public
                                    Required
                                    .false.
                                )
                        })
                    main
                    []
                    []
                )
        })
    []
)
```

This happens during AST -> ASR as the AST for this program does not contain the `AttrSave` attribute.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5714/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5714/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

#5690 
In this PR i have changed 
```fortran
integer, dimension(2) :: drratio=0 --------> integer,dimension(2) :: drratio
real, dimension(2) :: drratior=0.0 ---------> real, dimension(2) :: drratior
```
On ASR level 
Which prevents this llvm error 
```clojure
aditya-trivedi   lfortran    main ≡    lfortran integration_tests/array_init_with_scalar.f90 --show-llvm

#5707

- When no `program` line is used, we handle the `allocate` statement as a general `AST::FuncCallOrArray_t` node, which is also a `AST::expr_t`.

- Previously, we handled `AST::expr_t` only for `AST::Name_t` and skipped this case, causing errors.

- In the current implementation, we create the required `AST::Allocate_t` node and add to the program #5707

- When no `program` line is used, we handle the `allocate` statement as a general `AST::FuncCallOrArray_t` node, which is also a `AST::expr_t`.

- Previously, we handled `AST::expr_t` only for `AST::Name_t` and skipped this case, causing errors.

- In the current implementation, we create the required `AST::Allocate_t` node and add to the program #5707

- When no `program` line is used, we handle the `allocate` statement as a general `AST::FuncCallOrArray_t` node, which is also a `AST::expr_t`.

- When no `program` line is used, we handle the `allocate` statement as a general `AST::FuncCallOrArray_t` node, which is also a `AST::expr_t`.

- Previously, we handled `AST::expr_t` only for `AST::Name_t` and skipped this case, causing errors.

- In the current implementation, we create the required `AST::Allocate_t` node and add to the program #5690 
In this PR i have changed 
```fortran
integer, dimension(2) :: drratio=0 --------> integer,dimension(2) :: drratio
real, dimension(2) :: drratior=0.0 ---------> real, dimension(2) :: drratior
```
On ASR level 
Which prevents this llvm error 
```clojure
aditya-trivedi   lfortran    main ≡    lfortran integration_tests/array_init_with_scalar.f90 --show-llvm
; ModuleID = 'LFortran'
source_filename = "LFortran"

@drratio = global [2 x i32] zeroinitializer
@drratior = global [2 x float] zeroinitializer

define i32 @main(i32 %0, i8** %1) {
.entry:
  call void @_lpython_call_initial_functions(i32 %0, i8** %1)
  call void @_lpython_free_argv()
  br label %return

return:                                           ; preds = %.entry
  ret i32 0
}

declare void @_lpython_call_initial_functions(i32, i8**)

declare void @_lpython_free_argv()

```fortran
program main
    integer :: arr(1000)=1
end program
```

### Output

```console

real    0m5.837s
user    0m5.742s
sys     0m0.095s
```

For 10^4, the timing is well above a minute. The time is spent while constructing the `ArrayConstant` from the provided scalar. changes"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-12-15T12:50:21Z","updated_at":"2024-12-16T11:44:53Z","closed_at":"2024-12-16T11:32:25Z","author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Minimal Reproducible Example

```fortran
program main
   integer :: x=2
end program
```

### ASR

```clojure
(TranslationUnit
    (SymbolTable
        1
        {
            main:
                (Program
                    (SymbolTable
                        2
                        {
                            x:
                                (Variable
                                    2
                                    x
                                    []
                                    Local
                                    (IntegerConstant 2 (Integer 4) Decimal)
                                    (IntegerConstant 2 (Integer 4) Decimal)
                                    Save
                                    (Integer 4)
                                    ()
                                    Source
                                    Public
                                    Required
                                    .false.
                                )
                        })
                    main
                    []
                    []
                )
        })
    []
)
```

This happens during AST -> ASR as the AST for this program does not contain the `AttrSave` #5690 
In this PR i have changed 
```fortran
integer, dimension(2) :: drratio=0 --------> integer,dimension(2) :: drratio
real, dimension(2) :: drratior=0.0 ---------> real, dimension(2) :: drratior
```
On ASR level 
Which prevents this llvm error 
```clojure
aditya-trivedi   lfortran    main ≡    lfortran integration_tests/array_init_with_scalar.f90 --show-llvm
; ModuleID = 'LFortran'
source_filename = "LFortran"

@drratio = global [2 x i32] zeroinitializer
@drratior = global [2 x float] zeroinitializer

define i32 @main(i32 %0, i8** %1) {
.entry:
  call void @_lpython_call_initial_functions(i32 %0, i8** %1)
  call void @_lpython_free_argv()
  br label %return

return:                                           ; preds = %.entry
  ret i32 0
}

declare void @_lpython_call_initial_functions(i32, i8**)

declare void @_lpython_free_argv()
asr_to_llvm: module failed verification. Error:
Global variable initializer type does not match global variable type!
[2 x i32]* @drratio
Global variable initializer type does not match global variable type!
[2 x float]* @drratior

code generation error: asr_to_llvm: module failed verification. Error:
Global variable initializer type does not match global variable type!
[2 x i32]* @drratio
Global variable initializer type does not match global variable type!
[2 x float]* @drratior



Note: Please report unclear, confusing or incorrect messages as bugs at
https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues.

```

Minimal Reproducible Example

```fortran
module aoc_utilities

   type, public :: string
      character(len=:), allocatable :: str
   contains
      procedure, public :: to_int => string_to_int
   end type string

contains

   pure elemental function string_to_int(me) result(i)
      class(string), intent(in) :: me
      integer :: i
      i = 1
   end function string_to_int

end module aoc_utilities


program main

   use aoc_utilities

   type(string), dimension(2) :: vals
   print *, vals(1)%to_int()

end program main
```

Minimal Reproducible Example

```fortran
module main_module

   type :: primary
      integer :: x
   contains
      procedure :: call_fun => fun
   end type

   type :: secondary
      type(primary) :: t
   end type secondary

contains

   function fun(x) result(val)
      class(primary), intent(in) :: x
      integer :: val
      val = 1
   end function fun

end module main_module


program main

   use main_module

   type(secondary) :: vals
   print *, vals%t%call_fun()

end program main
```

Minimal Reproducible Example

```fortran
module aoc_utilities

   type,public :: clock
        private
        integer :: begin, end, rate
    contains
        procedure,public :: toc => clock_end
    end type clock

    type(clock),public :: clk

    contains

    subroutine clock_end(me, case_str)
      class(clock),intent(inout) :: me
      character(len=*),intent(in) :: case_str
      print *, case_str
      call system_clock(me%end)
  end subroutine clock_end

end module aoc_utilities


program main

   use aoc_utilities
   call clk%toc("5")

end program main
```

Minimal Reproducible Example

```fortran
module main_module

contains

   recursive subroutine sub(int)
      integer, intent(in) :: int
      procedure(sub), pointer :: proc => sub

      if (int > 10) return

      if (mod(int, 2) /= 0) then
         print *, "Odd: ", int
         call proc(int + 1)
      else
         proc => alt_sub
         call proc(int)
      end if
   end subroutine sub

   subroutine alt_sub(int)
      integer, intent(in) :: int
      print *, "Even: ", int
      call sub(int + 1)
   end subroutine alt_sub

end module main_module


program main
   use main_module
   call sub(1)
end program main
```

Minimal Reproducible Example

```fortran
module main_module

contains

   subroutine sub(int)
      integer,intent(in) :: int
      print *, int
   end subroutine sub

end module main_module


program main

   use main_module
   procedure(sub), pointer :: p_sub => sub
   call p_sub(5)

end program main
```

### Output

```console
(lf) saurabh-kumar@Awadh:~/Projects/System/lfortran$ gfortran test.f90 && ./a.out
           5
(lf) saurabh-kumar@Awadh:~/Projects/System/lfortran$ lfortran test.f90
ASR verify pass error: Initialisation of p_sub must reduce to a compile time constant.
  --> test.f90:16:31
   |
16 |    procedure(sub), pointer :: p_sub => sub
   |                               ^^^^^^^^^^^^ failed here


Note: Please report unclear, confusing or in

```
  integer,allocatable:: x(:)
  x = [1,2,3,4]
  print "(*(1X,I0))",x
  x = [5,6]
  print "(*(1X,I0))",x
end program
```
Lfortran 0.42.0 output:
```
Segmentation fault (core dumped)
```
Four other compilers gave this output, which IMHO is correct:
```
 1 2 3 4
 5 6
```

For deferred length character variables, GFortran allocates the required length on assignment. In the provided MRE, `a` is assigned to `b`, but `b` points to uninitialized memory as it is unallocated, and so segfaults.

From https://wg5-fortran.org/N2201-N2250/N2212.pdf, section 2.2

> 2.2 US 14. Automatic allocation of lengths of character variables
When a deferred-length allocatable variable is defined by intrinsic assignment, as in the example
character(:), allocatable :: quotation
:
quotation = ’Now is the winter of our discontent.’
it is allocated by the processor to the correct length. This behaviour is extended to messages
returned through iomsg and errmsg specifiers, writing to a scalar character variable as an
internal file, and intent out and inout scalar character arguments of intrinsic procedures, such
as in call get command(command).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2551140148/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

```text
Assignment behavior:

In the first program, the assignment a = b tries to copy the contents of b into a. 
Since b is unallocated, this operation attempts to access an invalid memory location,
causing a segmentation fault.

In the second program, b is initially unallocated, but the assignment a = "Hello" ensures 
that a is valid and allocated before any operation involving b. By the time a = b is executed,
b remains unallocated, but the compiler or runtime system has a more predictable state, possibly
handling the unallocated b gracefully (e.g., treating it as an empty string, depending on the compiler
implementation). However, this behavior is not guaranteed by the Fortran standard.
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2551159670/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Minimal Reproducible Example

```fortran
program main

   procedure(say_something), pointer :: proc
   proc => say_something

   call proc("Hello from proc!")

contains

   subroutine say_something(message)
      character(len=*),intent(in) :: message
      print *, message
   end subroutine

end program main
```

Minimal Reproducible Example

```fortran
module my_main_module

   type :: my_type
   contains
      procedure :: plus
      generic :: g_proc => plus
   end type my_type

contains

   subroutine plus(self)
      class(my_type), intent(in) :: self
   end subroutine plus

end module my_main_module

module my_second_module
   use my_main_module
end module my_second_module
```

working"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-12-18T22:06:39Z","updated_at":"2024-12-19T06:12:44Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Lfortran 0.42.0 gives wrong results from this program with every option tried. The issue arose from a comment by @certik in #5739. The loop variable ```i``` has the wrong value after one of the loops; which loop it is depends on an option.
```
  integer i
  do i = 1,2
     print "(A,I0)",'In loop 1 i=',i
  end do
  print "(A,I0,A)",'After loop 1 i=',i,merge(' good',' bad ',i==3)
  do i = 1,2
     print "(A,I0)",'In loop 2 i=',i
     if (i<2) exit
  end do
  print "(A,I0,A)",'After loop 2 i=',i,merge(' good',' bad ',i==1)
  end program
```
Output with no options or ```--std=f23```
```
In loop 1 i=1
In loop 1 i=2
After loop 1 i=2 bad 
In loop 2 i=1
After loop 2 i=1 good
```
Output with ```--use-loop-variable-after-loop``` with or without ```--std=f23```
```
In loop 1 i=1
In loop 1 i=2
After loop 1 i=3 good
In loop 2 i=1
After loop 2 i=2 bad 
```
Output (correct IMHO) from 5 other compilers: gfortran, g95, ifx, ifort, AMD flang
```
In loop 1 i=1
In loop 1 i=2
After loop 1 i=3 good
In loop 2 i=1
After loop 2 i=1 good
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5761/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5761/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2552873974","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5761#issuecomment-2552873974","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5761","id":2552873974,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCpMuHc6YKcP2","user":{"login":"kmr-srbh","id":151380951,"node_id":"U_kgDOCQXj1w","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/151380951?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh","html_url":"https://github.com/kmr-srbh","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-19T06:12:42Z","updated_at":"2024-12-19T06:12:42Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I think the issue here is that the `DoLoop` exits before complete iteration, but we always increment the loop variable after the loop is over. We need to handle the increment correctly for these cases.

```fortran
! Fortran code after applying the pass: do_loops
program main
implicit none
integer(4) :: i
i = 1 - 1
do while (i + 1 <= 2)
    i = i + 1
    print *, "In loop 2 i=", i
    if (i < 2) then
        exit
    end if
end do
i = i + 1
print *, "After loop 2 i=", i
end program main
```

Pushing a fix to handle the loop break.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2552873974/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Please go ahead.

If we look at the AST generated by this code using the `--show-ast` option:

```clojure
(base) saurabh-kumar@Awadh:~/Projects/System/lfortran$ lfortran test.f90 --show-ast
(TranslationUnit
    [(Program
        main
        ()
        []
        [(ImplicitNone
            []
            ()
        )]
        [(Declaration
            (AttrType
                TypeInteger
                []
                ()
                ()
                None
            )
            [(SimpleAttribute
                AttrAllocatable
            )
            (SimpleAttribute
                AttrParameter
            )]
            [(v
            []
            []
            ()
            1
            Equal
            ())]
            ()
        )]
        []
        []
    )]
)
```

we notice that `AttrAllocatable` and `AttrParameter` are present together. We need to detect this during variable declaration and throw a semantic error. See `visit_DeclarationUtil()` inside `ast_common_visitor.h` and try to handle this for both `AttrAllocatable` and `AttrPointer`.

Here is the test case for `pointer`:
```fortran
program main
    implicit none
    integer, pointer, parameter :: v => null()
 end program
```

We can consider adding a verification to ASR Verify like we do for `allocatable`, `pointer` conflict, but maybe we can do that later.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2552937045/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

working"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2024-12-18T22:06:39Z","updated_at":"2024-12-19T15:08:04Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Lfortran 0.42.0 gives wrong results from this program with every option tried. The issue arose from a comment by @certik in #5739. The loop variable ```i``` has the wrong value after one of the loops; which loop it is depends on an option.
```
  integer i
  do i = 1,2
     print "(A,I0)",'In loop 1 i=',i
  end do
  print "(A,I0,A)",'After loop 1 i=',i,merge(' good',' bad ',i==3)
  do i = 1,2
     print "(A,I0)",'In loop 2 i=',i
     if (i<2) exit
  end do
  print "(A,I0,A)",'After loop 2 i=',i,merge(' good',' bad ',i==1)
  end program
```
Output with no options or ```--std=f23```
```
In loop 1 i=1
In loop 1 i=2
After loop 1 i=2 bad 
In loop 2 i=1
After loop 2 i=1 good
```
Output with ```--use-loop-variable-after-loop``` with or without ```--std=f23```
```
In loop 1 i=1
In loop 1 i=2
After loop 1 i=3 good
In loop 2 i=1
After loop 2 i=2 bad 
```
Output (correct IMHO) from 5 other compilers: gfortran, g95, ifx, ifort, AMD flang
```
In loop 1 i=1
In loop 1 i=2
After loop 1 i=3 good
In loop 2 i=1
After loop 2 i=1 good
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5761/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5761/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2554512621","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5761#issuecomment-2554512621","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5761","id":2554512621,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCpMuHc6YQsTt","user":{"login":"kmr-srbh","id":151380951,"node_id":"U_kgDOCQXj1w","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/151380951?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh","html_url":"https://github.com/kmr-srbh","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-19T15:08:03Z","updated_at":"2024-12-19T15:08:03Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"@saikarna913 I will try to guide you through the debugging process when we encounter a bug like above. This bug has an important point to note - it is related to the use of a compiler option.

The first thing which we do when we encounter a bug, is to check the ASR. There are a few ways to have a look at it:
- `--show-asr` - Shows the initial ASR constructed from AST
- `--dump-all-passes` - Dumps files containing the ASR after each ASR pass
- `--dump-all-passes-fortran` - Dumps files containing Fortran code generated from ASR after each ASR pass

`--dump-all-passes-fortran` is convenient to see, but can sometimes be unreliable due to missing ASR node visitors. It also does not contain internal details about the code, like the variable type which the compiler got.

`--dump-all-passes` can be a bit tough to understand at first, but gets easy with time and provides much more information about the code.

The above code depends on the `--use-loop-variable-after-loop`, so we first try to find it's usage in the code and see what changes it introduces. This takes us to the `do_loop` ASR pass, which converts a `do` loop in Fortran, to a `do while` loop.

The above steps can be used to debug most bugs which we encounter. Some bugs are related to the way code is handled in the LLVM back-end, but the front-end can be responsible for the error, so it helps sometimes. In some cases though, the back-end code transformation can be solely responsible for the error.

Please let me know if you have more questions.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2554512621/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

#5707

- When no `program` line is used, we handle the `allocate` statement as a general `AST::FuncCallOrArray_t` node, which is also a `AST::expr_t`.

- Previously, we handled `AST::expr_t` only for `AST::Name_t` and skipped this case, causing errors.

- In the current implementation, we create the required `AST::Allocate_t` node and add to the program ```fortran
program main
   implicit none

   integer :: i

   do i = 1, 6
      print *,'In loop i = ', i
      if (i <= 4) then
         if (i - 1 == 3) then
            if (i - 1 == 2) exit
            exit
         end if
      end if
   end do
   print *,'After loop i = ', i
end program
```

is transformed to:

```fortran
! Fortran code after applying the pass: do_loops
program main
implicit none
integer(4) :: i
i = 1 - 1
do while (i + 1 <= 6)
    i = i + 1
    print *, "In loop i = ", i
    if (i <= 4) then
        if (i - 1 == 3) then
            if (i - 1 == 2) then
                i = i - 1 ! i decremented to make up for increment later
                exit
            end if
            i = i - 1 ! i decremented to make up for increment later
            exit
        end if
    end if
end do
i = i + 1
print *, "After loop i = ", i
end program main
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2554609619/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Please see `AST.asdl` for nodes with `unit_decl2*`. `AST::Module_t` can be handled in the same way as #5770. 

For others however, like `AST::Subroutine_t`, `AST::Function_t` we probably would need to extend the logic or do something different, as these can have self-referencing procedures. We have an issue open for this at #5735. Another case is `AST::DerivedType_t`, where we declare the procedure in the scope of the derived type, but have the subroutine/function declared outside.

need to handle this for language elements that support declarations. Please see `AST.asdl` for nodes with `unit_decl2*`. `AST::Module_t` can be handled in the same way as #5770. 

For others however, like `AST::Subroutine_t`, `AST::Function_t` we probably would need to extend the logic or do something different, as these can have self-referencing procedures. We have an issue open for this at #5735. Another case is `AST::DerivedType_t`, where we declare the procedure in the scope of the derived type, but have the subroutine/function declared outside.

_Originally posted by @kmr-srbh in https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/pull/5770#pullrequestreview-2517443260_

The other nodes which you mention represent the declaration of other language elements. The `Interface` node for example represents the declaration of an `interface` and `DerivedType` represents the declaration of a derived type in Fortran.

```fortran
module my_module

   interface i
      procedure say_something
   end interface

   type :: my_type
      integer :: n
   end type

contains

   subroutine say_something(msg)
      character(len=*), intent(in) :: msg
      print *, msg
   end subroutine

end module my_module

```
```console
(base) saurabh-kumar@Awadh:~/Projects/System/lfortran$ lfortran -c test.f90 --show-ast
(TranslationUnit
    [(Module
        my_module
        (TriviaNode
            [(EndOfLine)
            (EndOfLine)]
            []
        )
        []
        []
        [(Interface
            (InterfaceHeaderName
                i
            )
            (TriviaNode
                []
                [(EndOfLine)
                (EndOfLine)]
            )
            [(InterfaceModuleProcedure
                [say_something]
                []
                ()
            )]
        )
        (DerivedType
            my_type
            []
            (TriviaNode
                []
                [(EndOfLine)
                (EndOfLine)]
            )
            []
            [(Declaration
                (AttrType
                    TypeInteger
                    []
                    ()
                    ()
                    None
                )
                []
                [(n
                []
                []
                ()
                ()
                None
                ())]
                ()
            )]
            []
        )]
        []
        [(Subroutine
            say_something
            [(msg)]
            []
            ()
            (TriviaNode
                []
                [(EndOfLine)
                (EndOfLine)]
            )
            []
            []
            []
            [(Declaration
                (AttrType
                    TypeCharacter
                    [(len
                    ()
                    Star)]
                    ()
                    ()
                    None
                )
                [(AttrIntent
                    In
                )]
                [(msg
                []
                []
                ()
                ()
                None
                ())]
                ()
            )]
            [(Print
                0
                ()
                [msg]
                ()
            )]
            []
            []
        )]
    )]
)
```

A `procedure` declaration however, is handled through the `Declaration` node, so we need to handle only it. We use the `Declaration` node for declaration of many different language elements. They are handled during AST -> ASR mainly using `decl_attribute`. `AttrType` is found more often because it represents declaration of something which has a type, like an `integer` variable, or a `procedure` or a derived type variable or a `class` instance.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2557484635/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

- When no `program` line is used, we handle the `allocate` statement as a general `AST::FuncCallOrArray_t` node, which is also a `AST::expr_t`.

- Previously, we handled `AST::expr_t` only for `AST::Name_t` and skipped this case, causing errors.

- In the current implementation, we create the required `AST::Allocate_t` node and add to the program - When no `program` line is used, we handle the `allocate` statement as a general `AST::FuncCallOrArray_t` node, which is also a `AST::expr_t`.

- Previously, we handled `AST::expr_t` only for `AST::Name_t` and skipped this case, causing errors.

- In the current implementation, we create the required `AST::Allocate_t` node and add to the program - When no `program` line is used, we handle the `allocate` statement as a general `AST::FuncCallOrArray_t` node, which is also a `AST::expr_t`.

- Previously, we handled `AST::expr_t` only for `AST::Name_t` and skipped this case, causing errors.

- In the current implementation, we create the required `AST::Allocate_t` node and add to the program - When no `program` line is used, we handle the `allocate` statement as a general `AST::FuncCallOrArray_t` node, which is also a `AST::expr_t`.

- Previously, we handled `AST::expr_t` only for `AST::Name_t` and skipped this case, causing errors.

- In the current implementation, we create the required `AST::Allocate_t` node and add to the program    END SUBROUTINE CGAMA

end program main

working"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2024-12-18T22:06:39Z","updated_at":"2024-12-24T18:44:50Z","closed_at":"2024-12-24T18:44:50Z","author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Lfortran 0.42.0 gives wrong results from this program with every option tried. The issue arose from a comment by @certik in #5739. The loop variable ```i``` has the wrong value after one of the loops; which loop it is depends on an option.
```
  integer i
  do i = 1,2
     print "(A,I0)",'In loop 1 i=',i
  end do
  print "(A,I0,A)",'After loop 1 i=',i,merge(' good',' bad ',i==3)
  do i = 1,2
     print "(A,I0)",'In loop 2 i=',i
     if (i<2) exit
  end do
  print "(A,I0,A)",'After loop 2 i=',i,merge(' good',' bad ',i==1)
  end program
```
Output with no options or ```--std=f23```
```
In loop 1 i=1
In loop 1 i=2
After loop 1 i=2 bad 
In loop 2 i=1
After loop 2 i=1 good
```
Output with ```--use-loop-variable-after-loop``` with or without ```--std=f23```
```
In loop 1 i=1
In loop 1 i=2
After loop 1 i=3 good
In loop 2 i=1
After loop 2 i=2 bad 
```
Output (correct IMHO) from 5 other compilers: gfortran, g95, ifx, ifort, AMD flang
```
In loop 1 i=1
In loop 1 i=2
After loop 1 i=3 good
In loop 2 i=1
After loop 2 i=1 good
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5761/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5761/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"}}

```fortran
module main_module
   implicit none

   type :: toml_date
   end type


   type :: toml_datetime
      type(toml_date) :: date
   end type


end module main_module

program main
   use main_module
   type(toml_datetime) :: timestamp
end program main
```
```console
(lf) saurabh-kumar@Awadh:~/Projects/System/lfortran$ lfortran test.f90 --show-asr
(TranslationUnit
    (SymbolTable
        1
        {
            main:
                (Program
                    (SymbolTable
                        5
                        {
                            timestamp:
                                (Variable
                                    5
                                    timestamp
                                    []
                                    Local
                                    ()
                                    ()
                                    Default
                                    (StructType
                                        [(StructType
                                            []
                                            2 toml_date
                                        )]
                                        5 toml_datetime
                                    )
                                    ()
                                    Source
                                    Public
                                    Required
                                    .false.
                                    .false.
                                ),
                            toml_date:
                                (ExternalSymbol
                                    5
                                    toml_date
                                    2 toml_date
                                    main_module
                                    []
                                    toml_date
                                    Public
                                ),
                            toml_datetime:
                                (ExternalSymbol
                                    5
                                    toml_datetime
                                    2 toml_datetime
                                    main_module
                                    []
                                    toml_datetime
                                    Public
                                )
                        })
                    main
                    [main_module]
                    []
                ),
            main_module:
                (Module
                    (SymbolTable
                        2
                        {
                            toml_date:
                                (Struct
                                    (SymbolTable
                                        3
                                        {
                                            
                                        })
                                    toml_date
                                    []
                                    []
                                    Source
                                    Public
                                    .false.
                                    .false.
                                    []
                                    ()
                                    ()
                                ),
                            toml_datetime:
                                (Struct
                                    (SymbolTable
                                        4
                                        {
                                            date:
                                                (Variable
                                                    4
                                                    date
                                                    []
                                                    Local
                                                    ()
                                                    ()
                                                    Default
                                                    (StructType
                                                        []
                                                        2 toml_date
                                                    )
                                                    ()
                                                    Source
                                                    Public
                                                    Required
                                                    .false.
                                                    .false.
                                                )
                                        })
                                    toml_datetime
                                    [toml_date]
                                    [date]
                                    Source
                                    Public
                                    .false.
                                    .false.

                    []

                    .false.
                )

    []

```

As seen in the ASR above, when we create a derived-type variable (`timestamp`) from a derived-type (`toml_datetime`) which has a derived-type variable (`date`) as it's member, when we create the `StructType` for `date`, we add `2 toml_date` as the derived-type symbol for it. Later, when we create variable `timestamp`, the internal `StructType` gets copied as it is, so it is not present in the new scope, here `program`, and fails.

The issue arises due to the derived type symbol present. I had wished to do this refactor in parts, but I think we need to remove the symbol in the very beginning to refactor this. The `StructType` equality idea is clear to me, so I will try to do the complete refactor in one go.

I found that the above error can be reproduced only in a chained module compilation. An MRE like the above cannot reproduce this error even though the symbol pointed to is not present in scope. This can be the correct behavior as a `ttype` should not contain a symbol, so the above case will probably never arise, but I am not sure if we should worry about this.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2561364819/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

#5841

We convert the code:

```fortran
program main
   type :: m
      integer :: i
   end type m

   type(m) :: tt2
   tt2%i = 1

   call f()
contains

   subroutine f()
      print *, tt2
   end subroutine f
end program main
```
to 

```fortran
! Fortran code after applying the pass: nested_vars
module lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_
   implicit none
   type :: m
      integer :: i
   end type m
   type(m) :: lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2
end module lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_

program test
   use lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_, only: lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2
   use lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_, only: m
   implicit none
   type(m) :: tt2
   tt2%i = 1
   lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2 = tt2
   call f()
   tt2 = lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2

contains

   subroutine f()
      print *, lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2
   end subroutine f

end program test
```

#5841

We convert the code:

```fortran
program main
   type :: m
      integer :: i
   end type m

   type(m) :: tt2
   tt2%i = 1

   call f()
contains

   subroutine f()
      print *, tt2
   end subroutine f
end program main
```
to 

```fortran
! Fortran code after applying the pass: nested_vars
module lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_
   implicit none
   type :: m
      integer :: i
   end type m
   type(m) :: lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2
end module lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_

program test
   use lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_, only: lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2
   use lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_, only: m
   implicit none
   type(m) :: tt2
   tt2%i = 1
   lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2 = tt2
   call f()
   tt2 = lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2

contains

   subroutine f()
      print *, lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2
   end subroutine f

end program test
```

#5841

We convert the code:

```fortran
program main
   type :: m
      integer :: i
   end type m

   type(m) :: tt2
   tt2%i = 1

   call f()
contains

   subroutine f()
      print *, tt2
   end subroutine f
end program main
```
to 

```fortran
! Fortran code after applying the pass: nested_vars
module lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_
   implicit none
   type :: m
      integer :: i
   end type m
   type(m) :: lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2
end module lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_

program test
   use lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_, only: lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2
   use lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_, only: m
   implicit none
   type(m) :: tt2
   tt2%i = 1
   lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2 = tt2
   call f()
   tt2 = lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2

contains

   subroutine f()
      print *, lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2
   end subroutine f

end program test
```

#5841

We convert the code:

```fortran
program main
   type :: m
      integer :: i
   end type m

   type(m) :: tt2
   tt2%i = 1

   call f()
contains

   subroutine f()
      print *, tt2
   end subroutine f
end program main
```
to 

```fortran
! Fortran code after applying the pass: nested_vars
module lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_
   implicit none
   type :: m
      integer :: i
   end type m
   type(m) :: lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2
end module lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_

program test
   use lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_, only: lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2
   use lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_, only: m
   implicit none
   type(m) :: tt2
   tt2%i = 1
   lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2 = tt2
   call f()
   tt2 = lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2

contains

   subroutine f()
      print *, lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2
   end subroutine f

end program test
```

For modules, the implementation on `main` works correctly by importing the `Struct` into the new module, but it leads to incorrect behavior for `Program_t` as we try to import the `Struct` from the program into the module. 

With the current implementation, we duplicate the `Struct` into the module and remove it from the program.
