#5841

We convert the code:

```fortran
program main
   type :: m
      integer :: i
   end type m

   type(m) :: tt2
   tt2%i = 1

   call f()
contains

   subroutine f()
      print *, tt2
   end subroutine f
end program main
```
to 

```fortran
! Fortran code after applying the pass: nested_vars
module lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_
   implicit none
   type :: m
      integer :: i
   end type m
   type(m) :: lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2
end module lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_

program test
   use lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_, only: lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2
   use lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_, only: m
   implicit none
   type(m) :: tt2
   tt2%i = 1
   lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2 = tt2
   call f()
   tt2 = lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2

contains

   subroutine f()
      print *, lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2
   end subroutine f

end program test
```

#5841

We convert the code:

```fortran
program main
   type :: m
      integer :: i
   end type m

   type(m) :: tt2
   tt2%i = 1

   call f()
contains

   subroutine f()
      print *, tt2
   end subroutine f
end program main
```
to 

```fortran
! Fortran code after applying the pass: nested_vars
module lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_
   implicit none
   type :: m
      integer :: i
   end type m
   type(m) :: lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2
end module lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_

program test
   use lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_, only: lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2
   use lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_, only: m
   implicit none
   type(m) :: tt2
   tt2%i = 1
   lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2 = tt2
   call f()
   tt2 = lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2

contains

   subroutine f()
      print *, lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2
   end subroutine f

end program test
```

#5841

We convert the code:

```fortran
program main
   type :: m
      integer :: i
   end type m

   type(m) :: tt2
   tt2%i = 1

   call f()
contains

   subroutine f()
      print *, tt2
   end subroutine f
end program main
```
to 

```fortran
! Fortran code after applying the pass: nested_vars
module lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_
   implicit none
   type :: m
      integer :: i
   end type m
   type(m) :: lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2
end module lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_

program test
   use lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_, only: lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2
   use lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_, only: m
   implicit none
   type(m) :: tt2
   tt2%i = 1
   lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2 = tt2
   call f()
   tt2 = lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2

contains

   subroutine f()
      print *, lcompilers_created__nested_context__test_tt2
   end subroutine f

end program test
```

The docs in the pass say

```
This assignment and re-assignment to the variables is done only before and
after a function call. The same applies when a global variable from a program
is used by a nested function.
```

So the temporary is assigned outside the subroutine, used inside the subroutine and re-assigned back to the program variable after the call.

Here is another example:

```fortran
program main
   type :: m
      integer :: i
   end type m

   type(m) :: tt2
   tt2%i = 1

   call f()
contains

   subroutine f()
      tt2%i = 2
   end subroutine f
end program main
```

```fortran
! Fortran code after applying the pass: nested_vars
module __lcompilers_created__nested_context__main_
implicit none
type :: m
   integer(4) :: i
end type m
type(m) :: __lcompilers_created__nested_context__main_tt2
end module __lcompilers_created__nested_context__main_

program main
   use __lcompilers_created__nested_context__main_, only: __lcompilers_created__nested_context__main_tt2
   use __lcompilers_created__nested_context__main_, only: m
   implicit none
   type(m) :: tt2
   tt2%i = 1
   __lcompilers_created__nested_context__main_tt2 = tt2
   call f()
   tt2 = __lcompilers_created__nested_context__main_tt2

contains

   subroutine f()
      __lcompilers_created__nested_context__main_tt2%i = 2
   end subroutine f

end program main

Yes, my initial thought was to do the casting only, but it would have led to 8 byte integer index allocations for small strings also. Then `s_len` would have to be `int64_t` too and we would end up using more memory on the stack. 

We have many cases where we need to handle other integer types and the macro would lead to increase in the runtime library binary. So if the memory usage is not an issue, we can surely do the casting.

> It has bounds checking that cannot be turned off even in Release mode

We can handle incorrect indices by returning an empty string, but I think we need the bound checking to avoid undefined behaviors. We do this for other string operations like string slicing also. For string slicing, GFortran allows incorrect indices and prints random garbage values. LFortran handles this gracefully and returns an empty string, like CPython.

```fortran
program main
   character(len=5) :: color
   integer(4) :: k = 2000

   color = "green"

   print *, color(20:k)
   print *, len(color(20:k))
end program main
```

### Old ASR

```clojure
(TranslationUnit
    (SymbolTable
        1
        {
            iso_fortran_env:
                (IntrinsicModule lfortran_intrinsic_iso_fortran_env),
            use_statement_02:
                (Program
                    (SymbolTable
                        5
                        {
                            real64:  ;; ExternalSymbol imported using m_original_name "real64"
                                (ExternalSymbol
                                    5
                                    real64
                                    4 real64
                                    lfortran_intrinsic_iso_fortran_env
                                    []
                                    real64
                                    Public
                                )
                        })
                    use_statement_02
                    [use_statement_02_module]
                    []
                ),
            use_statement_02_module:
                (Module
                    (SymbolTable
                        2
                        {
                            dp:
                                (ExternalSymbol
                                    2
                                    dp
                                    4 real64
                                    lfortran_intrinsic_iso_fortran_env
                                    []
                                    real64
                                    Public
                                )
                        })
                    use_statement_02_module
                    [iso_fortran_env]
                    .false.
                    .false.
                )
        })
    []
)

I think it is correct and was implemented in such a way to avoid a name collision.

Here is an example:

```fortran
module main_module
   type :: my_type
      integer :: i
   end type

   type(my_type) :: my_var
end module

program main
   use main_module, only: my_var

   type :: my_type
      real :: r
   end type

   my_var%i = 1
   print *, my_var%i
      
end program
```

Minimal Reproducible Example

```fortran
module main_module
   type :: my_type
      integer :: i
   end type

   type(my_type) :: my_var
end module

program main
   use main_module, only: my_var

   type :: my_type
      real :: r
   end type

   type(my_type) :: my_second_var

   my_var%i = 1
   print *, my_var%i

   my_second_var%r = 3.14
   print *, my_second_var%r
      
end program
```

### Output

```console
(base) saurabh-kumar@Awadh:~/Projects/System/lfortran$ gfortran test.f90 && ./a.out
           1
   3.14000010    
(base) saurabh-kumar@Awadh:~/Projects/System/lfortran$ lfortran test.f90
code generation error: my_type doesn't have any member named r
   --> test.f90:112:4
    |
112 |    my_second_var%r = 3.14
    |    ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ 


Note: Please report unclear, confusing or incorrect messages as bugs at
https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues.

```fortran
module a
use iso_fortran_env, only: dp => real64
end module

program test_dp
use a
integer(dp) :: i
end program
```
This gives:
```console
$ gfortran a.f90 
$ lfortran a.f90 
semantic error: Variable 'dp' is not declared
 --> a.f90:7:9
  |
7 | integer(dp) :: i
  |         ^^ 'dp' is undeclared
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5870/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5870/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"}}

1. The installation instructions are misleading on what to do with a tarball after downloading it. They currently include as an example
```
tar xzf lfortran-0.42.0.tar.gz
cd lfortran-0.42.0.tar.gz
```
but I think the second line should have been
```
cd lfortran-0.42.0
```

2. I had actually replaced 0.42.0 by 0.42.0-835-ge375d9063 in the above as I wanted to see the effects of more recent changes. But the cmake command failed:
```
(lf) john:~/lfortran-0.42.0-835-ge375d9063$ cmake -DWITH_LLVM=yes -DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=`pwd`/inst . 
CMake Error at /usr/share/cmake-3.30/Modules/FindPackageHandleStandardArgs.cmake:233 (message):
  Could NOT find StaticZSTD (missing: zstd_LIBRARY zstd_INCLUDE_DIR)
Call Stack (most recent call first):
  /usr/share/cmake-3.30/Modules/FindPackageHandleStandardArgs.cmake:603 (_FPHSA_FAILURE_MESSAGE)
  cmake/FindStaticZSTD.cmake:15 (find_package_handle_standard_args)
  CMakeLists.txt:151 (find_package)


-- Configuring incomplete, errors occurred!
(lf) john:~/lfortran-0.42.0-835-ge375d9063$
```
What should the instructions have said?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5878/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5878/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2566328621","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5878#issuecomment-2566328621","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5878","id":2566328621,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCpMuHc6Y9xEt","user":{"login":"kmr-srbh","id":151380951,"node_id":"U_kgDOCQXj1w","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/151380951?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh","html_url":"https://github.com/kmr-srbh","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-31T10:33:08Z","updated_at":"2024-12-31T10:33:08Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"The `cd` command is indeed incorrect, but @harperjf I am unable to reproduce this issue on Ubuntu. or pull request for enhancing existing functionality"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-11-12T13:09:37Z","updated_at":"2024-11-12T13:09:38Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Minimal Reproducible Example

```fortran
program main
    implicit none

    integer, allocatable :: myInt
    myInt = 1
    
    print *, myInt
end program main
```

### Output

#### GFortran

```console
(base) saurabh-kumar@Awadh:~/Projects/System/lfortran$ gfortran ./examples/example.f90 && ./a.out
           1
```

#### LFortran

```console
(base) saurabh-kumar@Awadh:~/Projects/System/lfortran$ lfortran ./examples/example.f90 --realloc-lhs
reached else case
; ModuleID = 'LFortran'
source_filename = "LFortran"

@0 = private unnamed_addr constant [2 x i8] c"\0A\00", align 1
@1 = private unnamed_addr constant [5 x i8] c"%s%s\00", align 1

define i32 @main(i32 %0, i8** %1) {
.entry:
  call void @_lpython_call_initial_functions(i32 %0, i8** %1)
  %myint = alloca i32*, align 8
  store i32* null, i32** %myint, align 8
  store i32 1, i32** %myint, align 4
  %2 = load i32*, i32** %myint, align 8
  %3 = sext i32* %2 to i64
  %4 = call i8* (i32, i8*, ...) @_lcompilers_string_format_fortran(i32 2, i8* null, i32 2, i64 %3)
  call void (i8*, ...) @_lfortran_printf(i8* getelementptr inbounds ([5 x i8], [5 x i8]* @1, i32 0, i32 0), i8* %4, i8* getelementptr inbounds ([2 x i8], [2 x i8]* @0, i32 0, i32 0))
  br label %return

return:                                           ; preds = %.entry
  ret i32 0
}

declare void @_lpython_call_initial_functions(i32, i8**)

declare i8* @_lcompilers_string_format_fortran(i32, i8*, ...)

declare void @_lfortran_printf(i8*, ...)
asr_to_llvm: module failed verification. Error:
Stored value type does not match pointer operand type!
  store i32 1, i32** %myint, align 4
 i32*SExt only operates on integer
  %3 = sext i32* %2 to i64

code generation error: asr_to_llvm: module failed verification. Error:
Stored value type does not match pointer operand type!
  store i32 1, i32** %myint, align 4
 i32*SExt only operates on integer
  %3 = sext i32* %2 to i64



Note: Please report unclear, confusing or incorrect messages as bugs at
https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues.
```

GFortran handles this case through implicit allocation, where it allocates the size of the type implicitly when an assignment is made. We have support for this using `--realloc-lhs` flag, but it currently only supports array assignments. Another case where we have an error is:

```fortran
integer, allocatable, dimension(:) :: myIntegerArray
myIntegerArray = 1
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5370/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5370/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

Before this PR, we were adding the `ImplicitDeallocate` node twice, first in AST -> ASR, then in the pass. In this PR, I removed exactly the same logic from AST -> ASR as had been implemented in the pass, so we only remove redundancy, Currently, the case of `SubroutineCall` is still handled during AST -> ASR. In the next PR, I will port those changes here.

Talking of redundancy, we need to take care of the user allocatables being deallocated twice, first through `ExplicitDeallocate` node inserted during AST -> ASR, then through `ImplicitDeallocate` inserted in this pass. As `ImplicitDeallocate` does not throw an error for deallocating unallocated `allocatable` we do not get an error, but this still adds redundancy.

```fortran
program main
    implicit none
        integer, allocatable :: c(:)
        allocate(c(3))
        deallocate(c)
end program
```
```clojure
;; ASR after applying the pass: insert_deallocate
(TranslationUnit
    (SymbolTable
        1
        {
            main:
                (Program
                    (SymbolTable
                        2
                        {
                            c:
                                (Variable
                                    2
                                    c
                                    []
                                    Local
                                    ()
                                    ()
                                    Default
                                    (Allocatable
                                        (Array
                                            (Integer 4)
                                            [(()
                                            ())]
                                            DescriptorArray
                                        )
                                    )
                                    ()
                                    Source
                                    Public
                                    Required
                                    .false.
                                )
                        })
                    main
                    []
                    [(Allocate
                        [((Var 2 c)
                        [((IntegerConstant 1 (Integer 4) Decimal)
                        (IntegerConstant 3 (Integer 4) Decimal))]
                        ()
                        ())]
                        ()
                        ()
                        ()
                    )
                    (ExplicitDeallocate
                        [(Var 2 c)]
                    )
                    (ImplicitDeallocate
                        [(Var 2 c)]
                    )]
                )
        })
    []
)

program main
integer, allocatable :: d(:)
allocate(d(3))
end program
```
ASR after passes:
```clojure
...
(ImplicitDeallocate
    [(Var 2 d)]
)
(ImplicitDeallocate
    [(Var 2 d)]
)
...
```

The ImplicitDeallocate nodes are inserted in `array_op` and `insert_deallocate` passes. I think we have to remove this in `array_op`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/2763/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/2763/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5405","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5405/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5405/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5405/events","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5405","id":2665141713,"node_id":"I_kwDOCpMuHc6e2tXR","number":5405,"title":"real data type cause segfault or incorrect results on loongarch64 machine","user":{"login":"ouankou","id":1269492,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyNjk0OTI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1269492?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ouankou","html_url":"https://github.com/ouankou","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ouankou/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ouankou/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ouankou/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ouankou/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ouankou/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ouankou/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ouankou/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ouankou/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ouankou/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-11-17T01:34:54Z","updated_at":"2024-11-17T07:18:27Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I'm building lfortran 0.42  with LLVM 18 on a loongarch64 machine (CPU model: 3A6000).
cmake config is: ``cmake -DWITH_LLVM=yes -DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=`pwd`/inst .``
The build is successful. Then I tried the two examples from lfortran doc website: https://docs.lfortran.org/en/usage/.
Hello-world is good, but the varray one needs a little help.
The `real` element has some weird values, as follows:
```bash
(ssh)ouankou@dehya [ bin ] $ ./lfortran varray.f90 usev.f90
 sum is     0.00000000e+00
```
If adding `print *, A(i)` in the loop:
```bash
(ssh)ouankou@dehya [ bin ] $ ./lfortran varray.f90 usev.f90
3.23790862e-319
0.00000000e+00
0.00000000e+00
0.00000000e+00
0.00000000e+00
0.00000000e+00
0.00000000e+00
0.00000000e+00
 sum is     4.14452254e-317
```

On the same machine, GCC 13 and LLVM 18 worked fine.
```bash
(ssh)ouankou@dehya [ bin ] $ gfortran varray.f90 usev.f90 -o gcc.out && ./gcc.out
  sum is    72.0000000
(ssh)ouankou@dehya [ bin ] $ flang-new varray.f90 usev.f90 -o llvm.out && ./llvm.out
  sum is  72.
```

Additionally, I played with the iterative mode a little, and there's also some issue with real data type.
Assigning a value to a `real` type variable will cause segfault and terminate lfortran immediately.
```bash
(ssh)ouankou@dehya [ bin ] $ ./lfortran
Interactive Fortran. Experimental prototype, not ready for end users.
LFortran version: 0.42.0
  * Use Ctrl-D to exit
  * Use Enter to submit
  * Use Alt-Enter or Ctrl-N to make a new line
    - Editing (Keys: Left, Right, Home, End, Backspace, Delete)
    - History (Keys: Up, Down)
>>> real :: a                                                                                                                                                                                                                         1,10  ]
>>> a = 100                                                                                                                                                                                                                           1,8   ]
Segmentation fault (core dumped)
(ssh)ouankou@dehya [ bin ] !
```

Please let me know if any further information is needed. the changes in this PR, we create a new auxiliary variable for every `StructConstructor` we encounter. This has a drawback for cases where every `StructConstructor` is same - `call print_radius(Circle(1.5), Circle(1.5))`. I think we should ideally have only 1 common auxiliary variable and reuse it every time we encounter the constructor. I think it would require checking the equality of the current `result_var` value with the value of the `StructInstanceMember`. I do not know how much of an impact this comparison can have on performance. @certik could you please guide? @kmr-srbh this PR makes code that didn't run before to run? 

Yes. The code before had allowed creating only 1 temporary (`temp_struct_var__`) for `StructConstructor` in `stmt`. It failed for the linked issue, and for cases like:

```fortran
program main
    implicit none
    
    type :: Circle
        real :: radius = 1.0
    end type Circle

    type :: Square
        real :: side = 2.0
    end type Square
    
    print *, Circle(), Square(1.5)

end program main
```
```console
(base) saurabh-kumar@Awadh:~/Projects/System/lfortran$ gfortran ./examples/example.f90 && ./a.out
   1.00000000       1.50000000    
(base) saurabh-kumar@Awadh:~/Projects/System/lfortran$ lfortran ./examples/example.f90
code generation error: circle doesn't have any member named side
  --> ./examples/example.f90:12:24
   |
12 |     print *, Circle(), Square(1.5)
   |                        ^^^^^^^^^^^ 


Note: Please report unclear, confusing or incorrect messages as bugs at
https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues.
```

> Is there existing code that ran before, but now has an extra variable?

No, as the previous code worked for `stmt` nodes with only 1 `StructConstructor`. With the changes in this PR, we always create a new variable for every `StructConstructor`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2483618980/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

#4420 

We convert an `ArrayItem` like `x(loc, loc)`, where `loc` is an array, to:

```fortran
do __1_t = lbound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 1), ubound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 1)
    do __2_t = lbound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 2), ubound(__libasr__created__var__0__array_item, 2)
        __libasr__created__var__0__array_item(__1_t, __2_t) = x(loc(__1_t), loc(__2_t))
    end do
end do
```

### TODO:

working"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2024-11-20T15:26:42Z","updated_at":"2024-11-21T09:37:30Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"From https://fortran-lang.discourse.group/t/lfortran-now-supports-all-intrinsic-functions/8844/17:

```fortran
integer, parameter :: ten8=1e6
real :: tab(ten8)
call random_number(tab)
print *, 'sum= ', sum(tab), '  expected= ',0.5*ten8
end
```
The above works:
```console
$ lfortran a.f90
sum=     5.00064125e+05      expected=     5.00000000e+05
```
but when you change `ten8` to 1e7, we get a segfault (on macOS):
```console
$ lfortran a.f90
$ echo $?
11
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5427/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":2,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5427/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2490574272","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5427#issuecomment-2490574272","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5427","id":2490574272,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCpMuHc6UcyXA","user":{"login":"kmr-srbh","id":151380951,"node_id":"U_kgDOCQXj1w","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/151380951?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh","html_url":"https://github.com/kmr-srbh","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-21T09:37:28Z","updated_at":"2024-11-21T09:37:28Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"> In Fortran I think we should use stack arrays only if they can fit, otherwise we should use heap arrays. By default I think we should use heap arrays, and only use stack arrays if requested via a compiler option, possibly by default with --fast, but the user is then responsible to ensure the arrays fit.

Yes, we should be using the heap for this case. GFortran handles it similarly - if the array size is larger than the max var size allowed on stack (defaults to 64kB unless the user increases it at compilation), the arrays are declared on heap. If the value provided by the user exceeds the stack size, they again use the heap.

Reference: https://gcc.gnu.org/onlinedocs/gfortran/Code-Gen-Options.html

This was a very helpful example actually, it revealed a few more things that need to be looked into. I am opening issues for them.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2490574272/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Minimal Reproducible Example

```fortran
program main
   integer, parameter :: myInt=1e7
end program
```

### LLVM IR
```llvm
; ModuleID = 'LFortran'
source_filename = "LFortran"

define i32 @main(i32 %0, i8** %1) {
.entry:
  %myint = alloca i32, align 4
  store i32 10000000, i32* %myint, align 4
  call void @_lpython_call_initial_functions(i32 %0, i8** %1)
  %myint1 = alloca i32, align 4
  store i32 10000000, i32* %myint1, align 4
  br label %return

return:                                           ; preds = %.entry
  ret i32 0
}

declare void @_lpython_call_initial_functions(i32, i8**)
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5440/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5440/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

We convert 
```fortran
array_3 = array_1(:, array_2)
```

to
```fortran
DO i = lbound(array_1, 1), ubound(array_1, 1)
  DO j = lbound(array_1, 2), ubound(array_1, 2)
    array_3(i, j) = array_1(i, array_2(j))
  END DO
END DO
```

program main
    implicit none
    real :: A(2, 2), B(2, 2)
    integer :: P(2)

    ! Initialize the matrices and arrays
    A = reshape([1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0], shape(A))
    P = [2, 1]

    ! Slicing
    B = A(:, P)
    print*, rank(A), rank(B)

end program main

```

of #3770 

We now free allocatables inside `StructType` like:

```fortran
program main
    type my_type
        integer, allocatable :: int(:)
    end type

    type(my_type) :: x

    allocate(x%int(2))
end program

of #3770 

We now free allocatables inside `StructType` like:

```fortran
program main
    type my_type
        integer, allocatable :: int(:)
    end type

    type(my_type) :: x

    allocate(x%int(2))
end program
```

Along with this, we also free other scalar allocatables like `integer, allocatable :: int`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5486/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5486/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2498394586","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/pull/5486#issuecomment-2498394586","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5486","id":2498394586,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCpMuHc6U6nna","user":{"login":"kmr-srbh","id":151380951,"node_id":"U_kgDOCQXj1w","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/151380951?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh","html_url":"https://github.com/kmr-srbh","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-25T15:52:14Z","updated_at":"2024-11-25T15:52:14Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I feel the logic of collecting variables can be improved. of #3770 

We now free allocatables inside `StructType` like:

```fortran
program main
    type my_type
        integer, allocatable :: int(:)
    end type

    type(my_type) :: x

    allocate(x%int(2))
end program
```

Along with this, we also free other scalar allocatables like `integer, allocatable :: of #3770 

We now free allocatables inside `StructType` like:

```fortran
program main
    type my_type
        integer, allocatable :: int(:)
    end type

    type(my_type) :: x

    allocate(x%int(2))
end program
```

Along with this, we also free other scalar allocatables like `integer, allocatable :: int`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5486/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5486/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2498797683","html_url":"https://github.com/lfortran/lfortran/pull/5486#issuecomment-2498797683","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/5486","id":2498797683,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCpMuHc6U8KBz","user":{"login":"kmr-srbh","id":151380951,"node_id":"U_kgDOCQXj1w","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/151380951?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh","html_url":"https://github.com/kmr-srbh","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/kmr-srbh/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-25T18:53:51Z","updated_at":"2024-11-25T18:53:51Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"The current failure is due to this case:

```fortran
program class_11
    implicit none
    type t_1
        character(:), allocatable :: beta
    end type t_1

    type t
        class(t_1), allocatable :: alpha
    end type t

    type(t) :: gamma
    allocate(gamma%alpha)
    allocate(character(4) :: gamma%alpha%beta)

    ! Added manually to create MRE
    deallocate(gamma%alpha%beta)
    deallocate(gamma%alpha)
end program class_11
```

We have 2 things here:
1. We have a bug with handling `deallocate(gamma%alpha%beta)` when `alpha` is `ClassType`. This is unrelated to changes here.
2. We need to figure out the logic to deallocate implicitly exactly in the order above as `gamma%alpha%beta` is dependent on `gamma%alpha`. We need to figure out the correct dependency.

1 came up now when we tried to deallocate it implicitly. For now, we can disable this test, fix 1 and then work on 2.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/lfortran/lfortran/issues/comments/2498797683/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

of #3770 

We now free allocatables inside `StructType` like:

```fortran
program main
    type my_type
        integer, allocatable :: int(:)
    end type

    type(my_type) :: x

    allocate(x%int(2))
end program
```

Along with this, we also free other scalar allocatables like `integer, allocatable ::
